resume:
Priya Raman — Instructional Designer / Learning Experience Designer

Experience:
Learning Experience Designer, corporate L&D team (2020-present)
- Designed and built onboarding and compliance learning paths for a distributed workforce
- Partnered with SMEs to translate dense policy material into scenario-based e-learning
- Migrated legacy slide-based courses into an interactive LMS format
- Ran learner feedback sessions and iterated on course design

Curriculum Developer, online course provider (2017-2020)
- Developed self-paced courses in data and analytics topics for adult learners
- Wrote assessments and built rubrics aligned to learning objectives
- Collaborated with video and production teams on multimedia lessons

Skills: instructional design (ADDIE, backward design), Articulate Storyline, LMS administration, assessment design, SME facilitation
Education: M.Ed. Learning Design & Technology
jd: We are hiring a mid-level Instructional Designer for our learning-and-development org supporting a 4,000-person enterprise. You'll own end-to-end design of learning programs — needs analysis, design, build, and crucially measuring whether the learning changed on-the-job behavior. We care a lot about impact evidence: completion is table stakes; we want designers who instrument for knowledge gain, behavior change, and business outcome (Kirkpatrick L2-L4). You will partner with SMEs and business stakeholders, defend design decisions with learning science, and run iterative improvements based on assessment data. Strong candidates can point to a specific program where they measured learning impact, not just delivery. Tools: Storyline/Rise, an enterprise LMS, basic data analysis of assessment results.

history.q: Tell me about a learning program you designed end to end — pick the one you're proudest of.
For that compliance path, how did you do the needs analysis up front?
How did you measure whether learners actually gained knowledge, beyond completion?
Did you track whether the training changed anything on the job — behavior or a business metric?
Walk me through a time learner feedback made you redesign something specific.
How do you handle an SME who insists on cramming in content learners don't need?
What's your assessment philosophy — how do you write an item that tests understanding, not recall?
Take that scenario-based compliance course — what was the measurable impact, in numbers if you have them?

candidate_last_answer: You know, what I find really fascinating these days is the whole shift toward AI in learning. I've been reading so much about generative AI tutors and adaptive learning — I think it's going to completely transform our field. I actually just finished a great podcast series on the future of work and how skills are becoming the new currency. And it ties into this bigger conversation about lifelong learning, right? Like, the half-life of a skill keeps shrinking. I'm also really passionate about accessibility and universal design for learning, which I think doesn't get enough attention. There's so much happening in the space — it's honestly an exciting time to be an instructional designer.

top_question_traits: must-anchor-on: it's honestly an exciting time to be an instructional designer

expected-yield: pull her off the AI/future-of-work tangent and back to the specific compliance course — force one concrete impact number (pre/post score delta, pass rate, or a downstream behavior/violation metric) and how she instrumented it, since she has now twice avoided giving any figure
